attributes.stem:
A 23-year-old man presents after his friends noticed his eyes looked yellow. The patient has been obese since childhood and tried to lose weight on multiple occasions but failed. He recently heard about intermittent fasting as a good way to lose weight and started doing it five days ago. He does not smoke or drink. He has one sexual partner for the last four years and they use barrier contraception consistently. He has not had any recent international travel history. On physical examination, he has mild scleral icterus. His blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse 80/min and temperature 37.3oC. Abdominal and neurological examination is unremarkable. His laboratory results are shown below:
Complete Blood Count
Haemoglobin 158 g/L (128-175)
Leukocytes 7.2 x10^9/L (4-11)
Platelets 320 x10^9/L (150-450)
Liver Function Tests
Bilirubin 35 μmol/L (Normal: <21 μmol/L)
ALT 30 U/L (5-40)
AST 31 U/L (10-40)
ALP 67 U/L (35-110)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has mild jaundice with normal liver function tests (LFTs) which is consistent with the diagnosis of Gilbert's syndrome (GS). GS is a hereditary disease more common in men. It is mostly asymptomatic but may present with intermittent mild jaundice during stress. Common provoking factors for jaundice in GS are trauma, infection, surgery, fasting, and strenuous exercise. This patient recently started fasting which is likely to be the predisposing factor in this patient. GS occurs due to a mutation in the UGT1A1 gene which leads to decreased levels of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase. UDP-glucuronosyltransferase is involved in the conjugation of bilirubin. Decreased activity of this enzyme leads to elevated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia which manifests as jaundice. On laboratory findings, LFTs are typically normal with only mild elevation in bilirubin levels. GS is an asymptomatic condition and does not require any treatment.

Acute viral hepatitis due to Hepatitis A presents with fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain. It is also associated with tender hepatomegaly. This patient does not have a fever or positive abdominal findings to suggest viral hepatitis.

Choledocholithiasis presents with jaundice too. However, it is characterised by elevated levels of ALP. In addition, patients may also complain about postprandial pain in the right upper quadrant.

Haemolysis may present with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and jaundice. However, this patient’s haemoglobin count is normal. Hence, his jaundice is unlikely to be caused by haemolysis.

Wilson's disease is an autosomal recessive disorder characterised by elevated copper in the serum. It presents with jaundice, hepatomegaly, and neuropsychological symptoms. Wilson's disease is typically associated with elevated transaminases. This patient's unremarkable neurological examination and normal transaminases rule out Wilson's disease as a possible diagnosis.
learningPoints: Liver function tests are normal in Gilbert's disease.
references: Wagner K-H, Shiels RG, Lang CA, Khoei NS, Bulmer AC. Diagnostic criteria and contributors to Gilbert’s syndrome. Critical Reviews in Clinical Laboratory Sciences. 2018;55(2):129–39.
